This family consists of a diverse group of flowering plants that are mainly found in tropical and subtropical regions. The genus Euphorbia is further divided into several subgenera, and Euphorbia jessonii is likely a member of one of these subgroups. The classification of Euphorbia jessonii also reflects its evolutionary history and relationship to other plant species within the Euphorbiaceae family.

intro: Euphorbia jessonii, also known as the Japanese spurge, is a small, evergreen shrub native to East Asia, characterized by its thick, fleshy stems and small, greenish-yellow flowers. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiviral properties, which have been linked to various health benefits, including wound healing, pain relief, and the treatment of fever and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Euphorbia jessonii include euphorbol, a triterpene with reported anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity, as well as other compounds such as flavonoids and phenolic acids. In traditional herbal medicine, Euphorbia jessonii is commonly prepared as a decoction or infusion, or used in the form of a salve or ointment, to treat a range of ailments, including skin conditions, fever, and respiratory infections.
